content.text: User feedback on AI-generated outputs is fundamentally different from feedback on deterministic software. The same prompt can produce different outputs depending on model version, temperature, and context window state. Users judge quality subjectively, and their judgment shifts over time as expectations calibrate. Collecting this feedback at production scale — and turning it into reliable improvement signals — requires a deliberate
, and a pipeline that separates genuine signal from noise before any engineering work is prioritized.

attrs.alt: User feedback pipeline: Feedback Surfaces → Feedback Schema → Aggregation Pipeline → Signal Outputs, all joined by a trace + feedback store

content.text: User feedback collection for agentic AI products is the end-to-end system for capturing, storing, aggregating, and acting on signals that indicate how well AI-generated outputs meet user expectations. It spans UI surfaces (thumbs, stars, correction flows), schema design (what metadata to capture alongside each rating), sampling logic (which requests to capture when volume is high), aggregation (rolling up raw events into trends), and routing (sending processed signals to the right downstream consumer — prompt engineers, fine-tuners, or product managers).

AI systems degrade silently. Unlike a broken API endpoint that returns HTTP 500, a model that starts producing subtly worse answers triggers no automatic alert. User feedback is often the earliest detection signal.

Ground truth at scale. Human preference labels collected at production are the highest-quality training signal for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and direct preference optimization (DPO) fine-tuning.
A/B test evaluation. When experimenting with prompt variants or model versions, aggregate feedback scores are the primary business metric for variant selection.
Backlog prioritization input. Clustered negative feedback identifies which failure modes affect the most users — making it the most objective input to sprint planning.

Each surface has a different cost-to-signal ratio. Explicit surfaces generate labeled data but have low participation rates. Implicit surfaces generate high-volume behavioral data but require inference to interpret.
: lowest friction explicit signal. Participation rates typically 3–8% of responses. Best for tracking coarse sentiment trends.
: more granular but lower participation (~1–3%). Prone to positivity bias (most users who rate give 4–5 stars).

: user edits or rewrites the AI's output. The highest-quality signal — you get both a negative label (implicit thumbs-down) and a ground-truth correction. Participation is low (under 1%) but signal value is high.
: a 0–10 recommendation score shown at end of session. Useful for holistic session quality, not individual message quality.

: copy-to-clipboard, regenerate/retry clicks, session abandonment, follow-up clarification queries. These are high-volume but require careful interpretation — a regenerate click might mean "bad answer" or "I just wanted to see another option."
Every feedback event must be joinable to the full trace for that request. Without this, you cannot replay the interaction or understand what the model was doing when the feedback was given.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
import uuid

@dataclass
class FeedbackEvent:
    # Linkage keys (required)
    trace_id: str          # matches the trace stored in your observability system
    session_id: str        # groups a conversation
    message_index: int     # position of the AI turn being rated

    # Feedback signal (required)
    rating: int            # -1 (negative), 0 (neutral), +1 (positive)
    # For star ratings, map: 1-2 → -1, 3 → 0, 4-5 → +1

    # Enriched signal (optional but valuable)
    correction: str | None = None      # user's rewritten version of the response
    comment: str | None = None         # free-text comment
    feedback_surface: str = "thumbs"   # 'thumbs' | 'stars' | 'correction' | 'nps'

    # System metadata (set server-side, never trust client)
    model_id: str = ""                 # e.g. 'claude-opus-4-5'
    model_version: str = ""            # deployment version / git SHA
    latency_ms: int = 0
    prompt_tokens: int = 0
    completion_tokens: int = 0
    feature_flags: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    user_tier: str = "free"            # 'free' | 'pro' | 'enterprise'
    agent_steps: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # tools called

    # Timestamps
    feedback_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
Never trust metadata sent from the client. Set
in your trace store. Client-supplied metadata can be spoofed.
At low request volumes (under 500 requests/hour), capture 100% of feedback events. As volume grows, sampling becomes necessary to control storage costs and triage load. Key principles:
(thumbs-down, 1-2 star ratings, correction flows). Negative signal is rare and every instance is valuable.
: they are pre-labeled preference pairs ready for DPO fine-tuning.
for high-volume systems. Positive feedback is valuable for trend tracking but is plentiful — you don't need every instance.
: enterprise users' feedback often reflects complex use cases — consider 50% sampling for enterprise vs 5% for free tier.
: if a trace includes a tool call chain not seen in recent history, capture it regardless of rating to maximize diversity in the feedback dataset.

# Example: daily aggregation job (runs in Airflow / Prefect / cron)
import anthropic
from collections import defaultdict
from datetime import date, timedelta

def aggregate_daily_feedback(day: date) -> dict:
    """Roll up raw feedback events into daily signal summary."""
    events = fetch_feedback_events(day)  # from your DB

    # 1. Basic counts
    counts = defaultdict(int)
    for event in events:
        counts["total"] += 1
        counts[f"rating_{event.rating}"] += 1
        if event.correction:
            counts["corrections"] += 1

    # 2. Satisfaction score: (positive - negative) / total
    total = counts["total"] or 1
    satisfaction = (counts["rating_1"] - counts["rating_-1"]) / total

    # 3. Cluster negative feedback text for thematic patterns
    negative_texts = [
        e.comment for e in events
        if e.rating == -1 and e.comment
    ]
    themes = cluster_by_embedding(negative_texts)  # returns {theme: count}

    # 4. Volume filter: drop themes with fewer than 10 occurrences
    significant_themes = {k: v for k, v in themes.items() if v >= 10}

    return {
        "date": str(day),
        "total_feedback": total,
        "satisfaction_score": round(satisfaction, 3),
        "negative_rate": round(counts["rating_-1"] / total, 3),
        "correction_rate": round(counts["corrections"] / total, 4),
        "significant_themes": significant_themes,
    }
Not all negative feedback is actionable. A user who gives a thumbs-down because the AI declined a policy-violating request is
negative feedback, not a signal of model failure. Apply these filters before routing feedback to engineering:
: require at least 10 independent users to rate a similar output negatively before treating it as a systemic issue, not individual preference.
: apply a NLP classifier to negative feedback text to estimate whether the complaint is about model quality vs product feature gap vs policy behavior. Route accordingly.
: a theme that appears in negative feedback across many different sessions (not one user repeatedly complaining) is stronger signal.
: when running experiments, check whether negative feedback is elevated only in the treatment variant — if so, the experiment is the cause.
Storing Feedback Alongside Traces for Replay
The highest-leverage use of collected feedback is
: re-running the original LLM input through a new prompt or model version and comparing outputs. This requires storing the full trace — not just the final output — alongside the feedback event.
array sent to the LLM, including any system prompt, for each
so you can join feedback records to traces in O(1).
Retain raw traces for 90 days; retain aggregated feedback summaries indefinitely.
Strip PII before exporting traces to fine-tuning datasets or sharing with model providers.

The feedback UI itself should be A/B tested. Common variants to test: inline thumbs vs post-session survey, automatic prompt ("Was this helpful?") vs user-initiated, single-rating vs multi-dimension rating (accuracy, helpfulness, tone). Track participation rate and feedback quality (correction rate, comment depth) per variant, not just click-through rate.
makes it impossible to know what the AI actually said when the feedback was given. Always link before storing.
: a single thumbs-down on an edge case does not justify a prompt change. Require volume thresholds.
Optimizing feedback collection rate at the cost of user experience
: an aggressive post-message rating prompt every turn creates friction and reduces session engagement. Cap prompts to once per session.
: retry clicks and copy-paste events are low-cost to instrument and often reveal dissatisfaction that explicit ratings miss.
before the LLM call and log it to both the trace store and the UI response. This guarantees feedback events can always be joined to traces.
Always-sample corrections and negative feedback; sample positive feedback at a rate appropriate to your storage budget.
Run feedback quality checks weekly: verify that sampled feedback events have valid trace links, that PII scrubbing is functioning, and that confidence scores from your classifier are calibrated.
Use correction data as the first source for fine-tuning preference pairs — each correction is a (chosen, rejected) pair ready for DPO.

Feedback ingestion must be non-blocking — users should not wait for the feedback event to be processed before seeing the next response. Use fire-and-forget writes to an async queue (Kafka, SQS). The aggregation pipeline runs asynchronously on a separate consumer. At 1 million requests/day with 5% feedback rate, that is 50,000 feedback events/day — well within the capacity of a single Postgres instance with appropriate indexing.
: LangSmith, Langfuse, Helicone, Braintrust, Arize AI.
: Langfuse feedback API, Braintrust feedback SDK, or custom event schema.
: Apache Kafka + Flink for real-time, Airflow + dbt for batch.
: Anthropic fine-tuning API, OpenAI fine-tuning, together.ai, Modal.
values correspond to real traces before persisting feedback — this prevents injection of spurious negative feedback against arbitrary trace IDs.
Rate-limit feedback events per user session to prevent feedback stuffing (one user submitting thousands of negative ratings to skew metrics).
Audit feedback dashboard access: satisfaction scores and user comments may be sensitive — restrict to product and engineering roles.

. Your users are running implicit tests on every AI response and voting on the result. The feedback system is the mechanism that turns millions of individual votes into a ranked list of the most important quality problems. Your job as an engineer is to make voting frictionless (good surfaces), make votes interpretable (good schema), and make patterns visible (good aggregation) — then route the ranked list to the people who can act on it.
Every feedback event must be linked to a trace via
Always capture 100% of negative feedback and corrections; sample positive feedback according to storage budget and user tier.
Corrections are the highest-value signal: each is a (rejected, chosen) pair directly usable for DPO fine-tuning.
Apply volume thresholds and confidence filters before routing signals to engineering — individual ratings are noisy.
Set all system metadata server-side by looking up the trace — never trust metadata from the client.
A/B test the feedback UI itself: participation rate and correction rate are the key metrics for UI variant selection.
Retain raw traces for 90 days and aggregated summaries indefinitely; strip PII before any export for training.
